sentences.text: Well, in Europe, we are waiting very impatiently in-- in Italy, in San Vito what-- what-- what-- what will happen.
But the situation was such in-- in-- in Hungary-- the Germans occupied in Hungary, in March 1944.
And they did-- Hungary became-- became a lion's cage that-- that there-- there was no news from there, and so on.
And they had to wait for and opportune moment how to get-- get into Hungary.
And finally, on the last days, in August, 1944, all of sudden a national uprising took place in Slovakia under-- under the leadership of General Golian.
He was-- he was a colonel.
But then the-- the Czech government from-- from London the-- the [INAUDIBLE] government, said they promoted him to-- to a general right away.
And then, two days later-- two days later, we landed-- landed in Slovakia not very far from [NON-ENGLISH].
And from there, with the personal-- personal care and help of General Golian-- he organized-- he organized my way.
He-- he knew the importance of it because he checked out in-- in London also.
And then he was do everything, get everything you can in the fastest way.
And he organized-- he organized my-- my crossing the border with a trusted partisan.
And so, he led-- led me over in one of the-- one of the nights.
And into-- into [INAUDIBLE], at the time, because that part, you know, it was re-annexed at-- was given by Hitler to-- to [INAUDIBLE].
And then there, I-- I waited a whole week.
And with-- with the-- with the contact there, he was also a Jew.
And the reason he wasn't deported, because he was the only electrician.
And he has an electrical appliance store and was the only electrician in [NON-ENGLISH], a town of about 20,000, so they spared him.
And he was going in-- in-- into Budapest, and-- and on a Sunday night when they-- they called it a work train.
They-- they were outside workers-- went back to the-- back to the city to work-- to Budapest.
So he-- he led me in there right to the-- right to the-- the-- the custom.
And so, and everything in Budapest-- and then, we were working on the [NON-ENGLISH].
And this [INAUDIBLE] there.
And we came to the-- he came to a-- a hotel.
And he just whispered well, goodbye.
And he kept walking, and I went into the hotel.
It was about 4 o'clock in the morning.
And I had to-- to go to sleep.
So I-- I was in Budapest.
Usually, [INAUDIBLE] fed us breakfast and so on.
And I remember, you had this where to go and a name, address and name, your first contact.
Sure enough, in-- in about two hours time, working, I went and inquired-- inquired policeman.
So it's-- it's just to sort of-- see it-- it's a cover.
If you go to police-- minority, you must be [INAUDIBLE].
But anyway, I him about the direction.
And so-- and I came to the-- came to the-- the house.
I-- I found the house and knocked on the door.
There was a lady, about-- about your height, and so on.
And opened door.
And I-- I gave her a password-- [?
Robert. ?] And she froze.
She really got a shock, say-- saying his name.
And I said, well lady, let's-- let's not waste any time, and just pushed her in, pushed her in-- right in the chest-- let's go inside.
Robert. ?] That was the name of the-- of the British who-- who-- who briefed me in Bari, in Italy.
And he was the man who gave me this address.
He was once in-- serving in Hungary with the British embassy.
And so, and-- and close friends with this-- with this family.
And I'm looking for Mr.
Tigany So she told me-- was crying-- Mr.
Tigany has been dead for nine months.
Then-- then I got a shock.
Well, in that situation, like how I felt at-- at that time and in that moment I-- I don't know.
But it-- it--
She-- she was his wife.
Yeah.
And then, she-- she asked a-- a few-- few questions, how I know?
Well, I said, I met Mr.
Robert-- ?] Mr.
Robert ?] in Italy.
And she-- he gave me [INAUDIBLE].
And then, she calmed down.
And in-- within a very, very short time, that-- I-- I told her, well, look, here, I'm-- I'm in a mission here.
Can you help me?
Oh, she hold her head, and she looked left and right, and so-- and took-- in a little while, she said, yes.
What do you want?
So she-- she-- she realized.
She was an intelligence woman.
And I found out later, much, much later, only after the war, she was working for somebody else also, the-- in the other direction, toward Moscow.
She was an agent for-- for every-- for the-- for the allies, all-- all around.
She was an intelligence woman, and so on.
And then-- so we started-- started to work, you see, for the-- the assignment that-- that I had-- started to work on it.
And the very first contact was never mentioned-- the name.
But it was [NON-ENGLISH] General [NON-ENGLISH].
But I never met him.
But she-- she was the go-between.
And it-- it-- on and on to-- to other.
It's a long-- a long story.
So all-- all in my book.
And I hope people will-- will read it-- read the story And finally, when I get-- I got a promise of what we wanted.
We wanted a-- a landing ground in-- in Hungary for British parachutists to come in-- to come in.
And it was in collaboration with the Soviet Union, that the Soviet armies were pushing the-- the-- the German army.
And then, this operation was planned to-- to attack and stab the German army in the back.
So that was my-- my assignment, to find a landing ground for parachutists, commandos, and in Hungary.
And they found one-- Count [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know if-- if the name rings to you.
And then, oh well, at least I had that much.
Otherwise, it was a failure, because it was every-- everything [NON-ENGLISH] and the-- the air was [NON-ENGLISH] filled.
And then-- and then, I had to make a decision.
Well, she advised me to-- to get somebody, that she has a-- a trusted young man who could take my message back to-- back to Slovakia, to my mission, because there were four of us.
But the general could arranged it for-- it was impossible to get.
But I was the-- I was the main one, the-- the leading person of it, because I knew most around [INAUDIBLE] to-- and then-- then, I decided it-- it can't be done, because I can't track it down.
Can't-- and just too delayed, everything in one-- well, it's-- it's impossible, because I have to read the whole situation there and what are-- what are the possibilities for the-- for the mission.
So I had to-- to take-- take the chance myself to walk out.
And then, it took-- it was-- it was in-- in Sunday that I pocketed the [INAUDIBLE] things that I had, the Napoleon gold, some of it, and left the-- the house, left with Mrs.
Tigany.
It was about 200 [NON-ENGLISH], at the time.
You could buy a cow with Napoleon gold at-- at the time.

And then, the necessary papers what I had-- not so secured false paper, and out to the Western station to-- to find out-- to find out about-- about the-- the train schedule, out of-- back to-- back [INAUDIBLE], where I-- where came in.
That was-- that was on Sunday.
So that I can either come back to Mrs.
Tigany, or to spend the night somewhere else and go-- and go Monday.
But on-- I went into the restaurant on the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
It was [INAUDIBLE] boulevard in-- in Budapest, not very far from the Western-- Western station.

And at about 1 o'clock, when I was just finishing my dinner in the restaurant on the corner of a street in the main-- main boulevard there-- at 1 o'clock everything-- the radio stopped, the music on it.
And it stopped and announced that Miklos Horthy, the Regent of-- of Hungary, has a message to.
And he-- he said a, so to say, a bullshit talk.

It was actually a provocation, not a-- and not to help, that we are-- we are planning or considering to side with the other side, with the Allies, and so on and so forth, refuting the-- which would actually expose the Hungarian military leadership.
Because many of them took it for granted what was in the air already, waiting for the [INAUDIBLE].
They came to the service, and the Gestapo Nationals was right there.
And that was-- it happened at 1 o'clock when-- and then, I paid.
I just walked out to the street.
And there was, already, the street was full of people.
And they were debating, discussing, and yelling, and shouting, and so on.
I started to walk toward the station, the Western station.
And when I went into the station, people were like-- just like sardines, already, in the station.
And I decided, well, there's no-- no way back.
I tried to make a telephone call-- not to-- Mrs.
Tigany had no telephone call, but her friend, a wife of a doctor, who was another-- another contact.
I met her once.
And she mentioned Hannah Szenes' name.
But it's-- a side story is too much there.
But I heard it from-- from her.
And I had to pretend that I never hear-- hear the name.
But I head that.
I suspected that she's in here somewhere.
But by that time, she was-- she was in prison.
But anyway, when I walked into the-- into the station, bought a ticket back to [NON-ENGLISH].
And the train-- the train pulled out.
But it was so jammed on a second class ticket, that I was very early, lined up very early.
And I was sitting in the upholstered seats there.
And I was just there alone, yet, for a-- for a few.
And somebody walked into the-- walked into the-- into the porter there on the train and opened up the the English newspaper.
And I just looked at it.
And I had to make a fast, fast decision.
Shall I reveal myself?
Or what?
So I-- I just-- I just kept quiet.
And I noticed that he just-- just made a glance.
And when he saw there is no-- no reaction, or the reaction is that he doesn't want to react, he just walked out.
And even today, I don't know what-- what-- what was it.
Was it another British agent who do-- where they-- they-- they were covering me, or so?
And he wanted to identify himself and here?
Or was a-- was a-- was an-- a-- a counterintelligence provocateur?
So I just left it.
And-- and, you know, on the train.
The train pulled out.
And in [INAUDIBLE], there-- there was the-- the-- the nation's most-- what you call them-- a severe prison.
It was all-- all open already.
Oh, by-- by the time I got on-- on the train there, the-- the-- it was announced in the radio that [NON-ENGLISH] took power.
Yeah, October-- October 15.
And then, by the time we got to-- got up to-- they opened up-- they opened up the jail.
And they were-- they were dressing the-- the-- the people.
They were lined up.
Off with the-- what you call them-- the-- the prisoners outfit, and into-- into German uniforms.
Yeah.
And then, it-- it was in the-- in the morning.
Usually, about three hours-- three hours ride.
And it was about 6 o'clock in the morning, we arrived in there, in the [NON-ENGLISH] station there.
That station-- the station was full with all kinds of people on the train.
When some people went, they went even beyond.
They were mostly the well-to-do people, you see, escaping from the-- from the Red Army.
That's how it was.
They went to relatives, and friends, and so on.
And then, when it came day-- daylight, then I walked into-- into town, not very far, about five minutes walk.
And I just stood on one side of street and was waiting for-- for Mr.
Zolo who had this-- this electric-- electric store.
And when-- when he opened up the store, I waited a few minutes.
And then a few customers went in there with a couple of-- an old peasant women came in.
And I went and looked around when-- when the-- the woman went out.
And I just-- just turned-- I turned around, you see, and that, he-- he coming to-- to speak to you.
And all of a sudden, two-- two [?
Aerocross ?] soldier and a civilian in between them stepped right into the door-- Mr.
Zolo, you are under arrest.
And I just sneaked out right-- right-- right-- right by them.
He was-- he was under arrest, and [INAUDIBLE].
Well, he was-- he was a Jew, and-- and probably suspected that he was-- he was with the partisans, because when he was coming, and he-- he linked-- linked me up with the partisans there.
And well, that was [INAUDIBLE].
And I was going to go to-- to that farm where I came in, to that direction.
Well, then, I took off walking from there, towards the place where I-- I-- I-- in the-- that direction where I-- I came in, wanted to reach that-- that peasant-- that peasant [NON-ENGLISH].
But then, and in that direction, I was-- I was about two kilometers away from the border already, the Slovak border there.
And there was the border guards there.
And I looked around-- I looked around left and right.
Walking out there, I went through, luckily, the cornfields, and so to-- to-- to hide myself.
And there, on the other side of the road, there was a corn-- cornfield.

And from this side, I was crawling up, not very far, the elevated road, the-- the highway there, and looked around-- being on my belly-- looked left and right and saw absolutely-- absolutely nothing there.
And when I stood up and crouched down to run across the road, all of a sudden, I heard 'Halt' from my-- from my right.
And about 20 paces away, a German Luger and a soldier behind it.
Where it came from-- it came from, I don't know.
Never--
No.
But then, after, I figured they-- they must have-- must have followed me already from-- from somewhere.
They picked me up.
And then, he took me back to his station, to border guard there.
And there he [INAUDIBLE] this and this, and back to the village the-- the nearest village.
They locked me up in a-- in a wood-- woodshed, and so on.
Then-- then he came back about two hours later with a car-- and back-- back-- back into [NON-ENGLISH].
From there, they put me in the-- in the--
No.
No, no.
The paper-- the paper that they asked me, it was a-- a-- a paper that belonged to a person who was from that area there, who was called into-- into the Hungarian Army.
But instead of going into [NON-ENGLISH], he just stepped over to the-- over to the Slovakia, to the partisans.
And there, when they [INAUDIBLE], they-- they took all the papers, then put-- and they gave it to-- to kinds of people like myself.
And when they looked at this paper, they just smiled.
I had other-- other papers, but that was the one my-- that-- that was-- I'm going to use to-- to-- to get-- get back-- back across.
And they just smiled.
Well, because, they-- they knew the-- they knew the person.
See, the tragedy?
Ah.
And so, they did-- they put me up in a military-- military barracks in the-- in a-- in a cell.
And they put two armed guards on the-- and my-- my hands, they never took it off.
Behind my back, and there.
I just slept like this, you see, or what I could.
In the morning, they put me in a car with a Ukrainian in a-- in a Soviet uniform, a Ukrainian partisan they captured during the ride somewhere, operating.
Those were the real guys, [INAUDIBLE]. 1,000 kilometers ahead of Red Army.
And they dispersed into no-- no language, or nothing, but just [INAUDIBLE] and do something.
Kill a fascist, or blow up something.
Anyway, we were the two-- two-- two-- two prisoners, and then an-- an officer-- an officer.
I think he was in the rank of captain, who was then an-- an-- a chauffeur.
And we landed in-- landed in [NON-ENGLISH].
In between time, things-- small things has happened.
In [NON-ENGLISH], that was the Northern-- Northern station up the Hungarian counterintelligence.
And that was the nearest to them.
And they-- they placed us in there.
And they put me in cell number 1.
It was the-- it was the isolated-- isolated cell.
Next morning, they-- they led me out to the-- to the-- to the gate, to the door, into the-- but it was a thick wall, and so I couldn't-- I couldn't see the firing squad.
But I could see the-- that's how they placed me.
I could see the Ukrainian partisan on the other side of the-- of the yard.
And I heard of the, what you call, the command, fire.
And I saw when he collapsed and-- and so on.
Well it-- it was a sort of-- this is what you're going to get if you-- and then, they started to interrogate me there at-- well, it's a long-- long story.
Oh, [INAUDIBLE], yes.
The-- the very first thing was they slapped me in the face, left and right, on-- on the third question that they knew it was all-- all baloney, that I'm-- I'm lying.
And they pulled my shoes off, kneeled me in the-- in the corner there, and with a club, a rubber club on my-- on my sole.
And so, that was the initiation there.
And it went-- went on for a couple of weeks.
And then, I was put into another cell, when they were ready to-- they had to evacuate the prison, because the Red Army was in [?
Tokod ?] already.
And it's about, well, maybe 60 kilometers away.
And-- in the prison [INAUDIBLE].
Oh, my dear.
What-- what they wanted to know.
First-- well, first, my name.
Where you come from?
And well, we had a couple of story, you see, and an airtight cover story.
And I succeeded with that.
That was my luck.
I succeeded with it.
And-- but they-- they-- they beat me.
They beat me.
They-- you-- you-- you are a communist.
You are-- you are a-- you are the spy for the Bolsheviks, you are a [NON-ENGLISH] agent, a this and that.
That's what-- because I spoke so well-- well Hungarian.
And then, they ask.
And I told them, well, my-- my grandfather immigrated to-- immigrated to Canada.
You see, I'm a-- I'm a Canadian-born.
And in those early days, you know, the far Wild West, you know, Canada, all we had to-- I even knew the name.
And there really existed a-- a Hungarian teacher.
I-- I-- and I even met him.
Only time, when I was an organizer already in the Saskatchewan.
And so, it was airtight.
So they-- they began to-- to wave it right through the weeks of the interrogation, because I told them the straight story.
But there were some, especially one, Rose was his name.
That little bastard-- he keep-- would never-- he-- he never believed me.
But-- Did you have-- did you have any inmates in the prison, any other people in the same cell?
Well, it-- well it-- not in there.

But the only one, when they-- they put the transfer together, they put me into-- into another cell there with 12 partisans in Soviet uniform, Hungarian boys, who were in Hungarian-- were captured, you know, the prison where they trained them, who volunteered, trained them, partisans around Kiev there, in the training camp there.
And-- and they put me among them.
By God, I-- [COUGHS] I was-- I was at home.
[COUGHS] And the-- the-- the head guy was-- was a-- a cabinet maker, and so on.
And so, we spent-- we spent the time together to be shipped to-- to Budapest on the train.
Took about-- well, a six-hour train-- train ride from [NON-ENGLISH] to Budapest, about six-hour train ride, maybe less, took them three days.
You see--
No.
No.
No, no, no, prisoners' train.
A prisoners train.
And well, all-- all of the details, [INAUDIBLE] and details in there, all-- are all in my book.
But the partisans got the-- the railway ride, you see, here and there.
And we-- we came to that [INAUDIBLE] town there.
Then we had friends there, and one of the guards, who was a-- a guard.
But we-- we were in a-- in a cattle train.
A cattle train, that's how they shipped the prisoners.
One-- one of the guards-- it was a, really, a friendly, friendly guard-- guy, you see?
And by the time we got into-- oh, what was the name of that [INAUDIBLE] town there?
But anyway, his-- his wife was with-- with the three other-- other women who were waiting-- waiting already-- waiting already with food baskets, you see, with some-- some food.
And well, in the meantime, there was seven American pilots, who were shot-- shot don't over there and-- and was put in the same prison.
And we were in-- they were in the same group with us.
Finally, we arrived in Budapest in-- in the late evening.
And there was one-- one woman among-- in-- in our group, from [NON-ENGLISH] prison.
She was taken away to the-- to the [NON-ENGLISH] prison.
Hannah was also in-- in-- in a-- in a prison.
As a matter of fact, she was-- from that prison, she was transferred up to [NON-ENGLISH].
She was-- then she was shot.
So I met in my-- in my research then, I was-- I met some of these people, even that woman that Hannah was in the same-- same prison for-- for-- for a month.
And met a boy-- boy who was the neighbors from-- from school times.
And so-- so my book is pretty well-- pretty well-- But it-- please tell me, when you arrived to the-- to the prison in Budapest, what happened?
There, they put me in-- in cell 4.
That was the isolated cell.
And well, the very next morning I-- I found out there were other-- two Canadians and one British in one-- in one mission.
And the-- the commander was a-- a British captain.
But-- but the other two were-- were my-- my personal friends and comrades right from-- from Canada.
One of them, [INAUDIBLE].
Well, he was-- once he was a-- a general secretary of [INAUDIBLE] organization.
And they were in cell 1, and I was in-- in cell 4.
And then all-- all around me, the next cell was a-- was a woman's cell, where they-- where I had to go through where-- where they-- they tortured and-- and raped.
And so, some Russian girls who-- who were radio operators, a couple days later, when they brought in a young Jewish woman-- and [INAUDIBLE], it is horrible, horrible stories.
And you-- you-- you heard it all, you see, all this torturing, and-- and--
Well, in the-- in the prison something-- something unusual-- unusual happened.

It-- it was a-- was a [INAUDIBLE] thing that-- at one night-- at one day, when I was-- I was up for interrogation, which was on first floor, the-- the interrogation room-- and also, the severe interrogation room, especially when they-- when they really tortured.
In this interrogation room, they just slapped you.
In the-- in the other room, when there-- there was-- they finally broke the spine, the Gestapo, because they were there in the same-- same prison.
And one day, in the afternoon-- it was the one only afternoon when they interrogated me in the afternoon.
Usually, it was always in the morning.
I found a guy in-- in my-- in my cell.
He was-- he was about my-- about my height, but he was thinner.
And he was-- he was dressed in-- in a partisan uniform, the same kind of uniform that I saw on those boys that I-- I met in the-- in the [NON-ENGLISH] prison.
So he pretended that he-- he's a-- he's a partisan prisoner.
But after the third question, the penny dropped.
Because finally, he asked me that-- if I could give him-- that he was also-- was also captured so and so as a-- as a partisan coming back from-- from the Soviet Union.
And then, his time is over now.
He said, they didn't strike me right away.
They-- they don't let the all kind of people to go.
But anyway-- anyway, I-- I didn't catch on right away.
But that-- that was-- that was another question.
And the third question is he's-- he's released.
He-- he-- he's going to be free in two-- two days from now.
And he-- he is from-- from Transylvania.
Yeah, but don't--
